notes.note: Vennonius was a Roman annalist whose history Cicero elsewhere rates poorly; he wants the volume to consult. No entity record exists for him in this corpus.

notes.anchor: that debt of mine from Caesar
notes.note: A sum Caesar owed Cicero (or a credit standing in Caesar's name), which Cicero is trying to realise without loss. The three options that follow are ways of recovering or assigning the debt, a recurring financial worry of these years.

A spear (hasta) planted in the ground marked a public auction, especially of confiscated or state property; "buying at the spear" meant acquiring goods this way. Cicero finds the option degrading as well as unprofitable.

notes.anchor: when will that Metonic year of his come round
notes.note: Meton of Athens devised a nineteen-year lunisolar cycle; "a Metonic year" is Cicero's sardonic way of saying the contractor's one-year term will never actually arrive. The joke turns a long astronomical period into a byword for indefinite delay.
